summary:
The Tây Sơn Rebellion (1771–1802 AD) was a sweeping peasant uprising that overthrew both the Trịnh lords of northern Vietnam and the Nguyễn lords of the south, reunified the country under the brilliant general Nguyễn Huệ (Emperor Quang Trung), and even repelled a massive Chinese Qing invasion — only to be itself overthrown by the surviving Nguyễn prince Nguyễn Ánh, who established the [[event:3249|Nguyễn dynasty]] with French assistance. The rebellion began in the western highlands of Quy Nhơn, led by three brothers from the Tây Sơn village, drawing on grievances against heavy taxation, corrupt local lords, and the exploitation of merchants. At its height, the Tây Sơn movement controlled all of Vietnam for the first time in over two centuries.

The most dramatic moment of the rebellion was Nguyễn Huệ's defeat of the Chinese invasion of 1789 — the [[event:2337|Qing]] had sent 200,000 troops to restore the Lê dynasty at the request of the last Lê emperor. In a lightning winter campaign, Nguyễn Huệ assembled his forces, marched north, and destroyed the Chinese army at the Battle of Đống Đa (January 1789) in a surprise attack during the Tết (Lunar New Year) holiday — one of Vietnam's greatest military victories, still celebrated annually. Emperor Quang Trung died suddenly in 1792, leaving the Tây Sơn state weakened by succession conflicts; the surviving Nguyễn prince Nguyễn Ánh, supported by French missionary Bishop Pigneau de Béhaine and French volunteers, gradually reconquered Vietnam and established Huế as the imperial capital of the Nguyễn dynasty in 1802.
